one_liner: A 48-year-old obese female presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting 2 hours after eating a large meal.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient presents with severe epigastric pain that began suddenly approximately 6 hours ago, roughly 2 hours after eating a large holiday dinner. The pain is constant, boring, rated 9/10, and radiates straight through to her mid-back. She has had persistent nausea with four episodes of vomiting (initially food contents, then bilious). She reports that lying flat worsens the pain, while leaning forward provides slight relief. She has had similar but milder episodes of upper abdominal pain after fatty meals over the past year, usually lasting 1-2 hours and self-resolving. This episode is far more severe and has not improved. She denies alcohol use, recent medication changes, fever, jaundice, diarrhea, or hematemesis.
stage2.pmh: Obesity, known gallstones (incidentally found on ultrasound 2 years ago, declined surgery), hypertension, prediabetes
stage2.medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg daily
stage2.family_history: Mother with gallstones and diabetes. Father with coronary artery disease.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. No alcohol use. Works as a schoolteacher. Married with 2 children. No illicit drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Obese female in significant distress, restless, unable to find a comfortable position, leaning forward. HEENT: **Mild scleral icterus bilaterally**; **Dry mucous membranes**. Neck: Supple. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm**; No murmurs. Pulmonary: **Decreased breath sounds at the left base**; **Dullness to percussion at the left base**. Abdomen: **Epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding**; No rebound; No rigidity; **Mildly distended**; **Hypoactive bowel sounds**; No palpable mass; **Mild tenderness in the RUQ as well**; No Grey Turner or Cullen sign. Rectal: Guaiac negative. Extremities: **Trace bilateral pedal edema**. Skin: **Mild jaundice**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated (>3x upper limit of normal), diagnostic of acute pancreatitis
Markedly elevated, consistent with acute pancreatitis
Elevated, suggesting transient or ongoing biliary obstruction from gallstone passage
Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia consistent with biliary obstruction
Significantly elevated; ALT >150 U/L has 95% positive predictive value for this condition
Elevated, cholestatic pattern supporting biliary obstruction
Normal; hypocalcemia would be a poor prognostic sign (Ranson criteria)
Normal, no evidence of tissue hypoperfusion

imaging_results.result: Multiple gallstones present, largest 1.2 cm. Gallbladder wall mildly thickened (4 mm). Common bile duct dilated at 9 mm (normal <6 mm). Possible shadowing stone at the distal CBD, though partially obscured by bowel gas. Pancreas not well visualized due to body habitus.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Gallstones with CBD dilation strongly support this condition. Possible distal CBD stone may require ERCP.

imaging_results.result: Pancreas is diffusely enlarged and edematous with peripancreatic fat stranding and small amount of peripancreatic fluid. No pancreatic necrosis. No pseudocyst. Gallstones noted. CBD mildly dilated. Small left pleural effusion.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Findings consistent with acute interstitial edematous pancreatitis without necrosis. CT severity index is low, suggesting mild disease radiographically.

low_yield_tests: ERCP as initial diagnostic test (therapeutic, not first-line diagnostic)
Serum IgG4 (for autoimmune pancreatitis, not acute gallstone pancreatitis)
stage3.final_clue: Lipase is markedly elevated at 2,840 U/L. ALT of 286 U/L strongly predicts gallstone etiology. Ultrasound reveals gallstones and a dilated CBD (9 mm). CT shows acute edematous pancreatitis without necrosis. This is acute gallstone pancreatitis requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation, pain management, and cholecystectomy during the same admission.

stage5.final_clue: Lipase is markedly elevated at 2,840 U/L. ALT of 286 U/L strongly predicts gallstone etiology.

debrief.classic_presentation: Severe, constant epigastric pain radiating to the back, worse supine and improved leaning forward, with nausea and vomiting after a fatty meal. Gallstones and alcohol are the two most common causes (together accounting for ~80% of cases). Gallstone pancreatitis is characterized by elevated liver enzymes (particularly ALT >150 U/L, which has 95% PPV for gallstone etiology) and gallstones on ultrasound. Jaundice may be present if CBD obstruction is ongoing.
debrief.pathophysiology: Gallstone pancreatitis occurs when a gallstone migrates from the gallbladder through the cystic duct and impacts at the ampulla of Vater, obstructing the common channel for the pancreatic and bile ducts. This causes reflux of bile into the pancreatic duct and/or obstruction of pancreatic secretion outflow. Premature activation of trypsinogen to trypsin within the pancreas triggers autodigestion, leading to acinar cell injury, edema, and a cascade of inflammatory mediators (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6). Severe cases progress to pancreatic necrosis, SIRS, and multi-organ failure.
debrief.treatment_overview: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (goal-directed, lactated Ringer preferred). Pain management (IV opioids, NSAIDs). NPO initially, then early oral feeding as tolerated (within 24-48 hours if tolerated). Severity assessment (BISAP, Ranson, APACHE II). If signs of cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction, urgent ERCP within 24 hours. Same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy after pancreatitis resolves to prevent recurrence (30-50% recurrence rate if cholecystectomy deferred). If severe necrotizing pancreatitis, cholecystectomy may be delayed. No role for prophylactic antibiotics in the absence of infected necrosis.
high_yield_pearls: ALT >150 U/L has a 95% positive predictive value for gallstone etiology of acute pancreatitis.
Lipase is more sensitive and specific than amylase for acute pancreatitis; a value >3x the upper limit of normal is diagnostic.
Same-admission cholecystectomy is recommended for mild gallstone pancreatitis to prevent recurrence (recurrence rate 30-50% within 6 weeks if deferred).
Urgent ERCP (within 24 hours) is indicated only if there is concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction, not for all gallstone pancreatitis.
Grey Turner sign (flank ecchymosis) and Cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis) indicate retroperitoneal hemorrhage in severe necrotizing pancreatitis but are rare and late findings.
sources: AGA Guidelines on Initial Management of Acute Pancreatitis (Gastroenterology 2018;154:1096-1101) — https://gastro.org/practice-guidance/
IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for Management of Acute Pancreatitis (Pancreatology 2013;13:e1-e15) — https://www.psychiatry.org/psychiatrists/practice/clinical-practice-guidelines
Schwartz's Principles of Surgery, 11th Edition, Ch. 33 — https://accessmedicine.mhmedical.com/book.aspx?bookID=2576
